bins.name: Cost Causation Applied Wrongly
bins.desc: Maven Solutions opposes FirstEnergy's expansion-rate proposal as a misapplication of cost-causation doctrine. The filing argues that triggering a transmission upgrade does not equal bearing its full cost; proper allocation requires a benefit study that accounts for regional reliability, congestion relief, and future load service. The proposal also secures transmission-owner revenue while shifting all risk to the customer, and its reallocation mechanism is unspecified.
quotes: The natural-gas analogy is directionally useful and structurally false.

Electric transmission is a meshed alternating-current network in which power flows simultaneously over all transmission paths according to electrical characteristics—not according to contractual designations.

A transmission facility built to interconnect a data center may simultaneously improve regional reliability, reduce congestion, increase transfer capability, support future load service, and enable generation deliverability for parties who had no part in the interconnection request.
The proposal does not eliminate the bottleneck. It monetizes it.

FirstEnergy's framework guarantees the transmission owner's cost recovery, collateralizes the transmission owner's investment, and shifts all demand-forecast, utilization, and cancellation risk from the utility to the customer.
"Triggered by a data center" is not the same as "benefits only a data center." FirstEnergy wants the causation label without doing the beneficiary math.
The Commission cannot approve a framework that announces reallocation as a concept without specifying it as a mechanism.

Political pledges and filed-rate obligations are different instruments. The Commission should not confuse a White House press event with the evidentiary record required to support a just-and-reasonable rate under FPA §§ 205–206.
FirstEnergy's expansion-rate proposal is, in part, a direct response to that litigation. The filing says: do not block the project—fix the rate design.
A rate-design framework that assigns capital costs without addressing dynamic-load performance creates a dangerous half-solution.

Collateral requirements must be proportional to risk, not designed to exclude smaller competitors. A framework that only trillion-dollar hyperscalers can satisfy entrenches the largest firms while pricing out smaller developers, manufacturers, and emerging industries.
The gas analogy is useful as a directional signal. It is false as a one-to-one regulatory mapping.

bins.name: Benefit Study Before Assignment
bins.desc: Maven Solutions supports requiring a public benefit study before any direct cost assignment, covering congestion relief, reliability improvement, transfer capability, generation deliverability, and avoided-cost benefits. The filing also ties this to NERC reliability conditions: computational-load modeling and dynamic-load simulations must be satisfied before a customer receives transmission service under any expansion rate.
quotes: A transmission facility built to interconnect a data center may simultaneously improve regional reliability, reduce congestion, increase transfer capability, support future load service, and enable generation deliverability for parties who had no part in the interconnection request.
"Triggered by a data center" is not the same as "benefits only a data center." FirstEnergy wants the causation label without doing the beneficiary math.
A rate-design framework that assigns capital costs without addressing dynamic-load performance creates a dangerous half-solution.

The Commission should not adopt any cost-allocation framework for large computational loads without requiring the applicant to satisfy NERC's emerging reliability obligations as a condition of receiving transmission service.

bins.name: Community Hosting Accountability
bins.desc: Maven Solutions argues that any cost-allocation framework must include community hosting standards, not just ratepayer protection. The filing documents 268 opposition groups across 37 states and argues that assigning transmission costs without addressing environmental impact, water use, workforce participation, and tax-revenue transparency will deepen rather than resolve community opposition.
quotes: Maven Solutions' research documents the national scale of community opposition to data-center development: 268 local groups across 37 states, representing over 360,000 members, coordinated through organizations including the Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development.
A framework that assigns transmission costs while ignoring the communities standing next to the transmission facilities will not resolve that opposition. It will deepen it.

bins.name: Gas Model Structurally Inapplicable
bins.desc: Maven Solutions identifies the gas-pipeline analogy as the structural flaw in FirstEnergy's filing. AC transmission flows across all paths simultaneously according to electrical physics, not contractual designations, so a contract-path pricing model imported from a commodity pipeline industry does not map onto a meshed, reliability-critical network.
quotes: The natural-gas analogy is directionally useful and structurally false.

Electric transmission is a meshed alternating-current network in which power flows simultaneously over all transmission paths according to electrical characteristics—not according to contractual designations.
The gas analogy is useful as a directional signal. It is false as a one-to-one regulatory mapping.

bins.name: Proposal Monetizes Monopoly Position
bins.desc: Maven Solutions argues that FirstEnergy's framework converts incumbent monopoly control of transmission into a FERC-approved revenue extraction mechanism. The proposal guarantees utility cost recovery and collateralizes utility investment while presenting itself as consumer protection. The filing also discloses that the proposal responds to FirstEnergy's own rate litigation, flagging a conflict of interest.
quotes: The proposal does not eliminate the bottleneck. It monetizes it.

FirstEnergy's framework guarantees the transmission owner's cost recovery, collateralizes the transmission owner's investment, and shifts all demand-forecast, utilization, and cancellation risk from the utility to the customer.
FirstEnergy's expansion-rate proposal is, in part, a direct response to that litigation. The filing says: do not block the project—fix the rate design.

bins.name: Reallocation Left Unspecified
bins.desc: FirstEnergy acknowledges that expansion facilities may eventually serve the broader grid and gestures at annual reallocation, but leaves frequency, methodology, and enforcement entirely undefined. Maven Solutions argues the Commission cannot approve a framework that names reallocation as a concept without specifying it as an enforceable mechanism.
quotes: The Commission cannot approve a framework that announces reallocation as a concept without specifying it as a mechanism.

bins.name: White House Pledge Not a Rate Basis
bins.desc: Maven Solutions argues the White House Ratepayer Protection Pledge is a voluntary political commitment by seven named companies, not a FERC tariff or statutory cost-allocation rule. Converting that pledge into a mandatory cost-recovery mechanism applied to all hyperscale customers conflates a press event with the evidentiary record required under FPA sections 205 and 206.
quotes: Political pledges and filed-rate obligations are different instruments. The Commission should not confuse a White House press event with the evidentiary record required to support a just-and-reasonable rate under FPA §§ 205–206.

bins.name: Reliability Conditions Required
bins.desc: Maven Solutions points to NERC's March 2026 Gap Assessment and May 2026 Level 3 Essential Action Alert finding existing standards inadequate for computational loads. The filing argues that any cost-allocation framework must condition transmission service on satisfying NERC's emerging reliability obligations, including dynamic-load modeling, telemetry, and ride-through capability.
quotes: A rate-design framework that assigns capital costs without addressing dynamic-load performance creates a dangerous half-solution.

The Commission should not adopt any cost-allocation framework for large computational loads without requiring the applicant to satisfy NERC's emerging reliability obligations as a condition of receiving transmission service.

bins.name: Collateral Standards Must Be Scaled
bins.desc: Maven Solutions accepts that collateral requirements have a role in managing project risk but argues they must be proportional rather than set at levels only large hyperscalers can meet. A framework calibrated to trillion-dollar firms would entrench the largest companies and price out smaller developers, manufacturers, and emerging industries.
quotes: Collateral requirements must be proportional to risk, not designed to exclude smaller competitors. A framework that only trillion-dollar hyperscalers can satisfy entrenches the largest firms while pricing out smaller developers, manufacturers, and emerging industries.
